text: The surgical setup includes a ten-millimeter port placed at the umbilicus, another ten-millimeter port positioned at the left inguinal region, and two five-millimeter ports.
The surgical setup includes a ten millimeter port from the right inguinal region and another port from the suprapubic area.
The left fallopian tube and left ovary are visibly attached to the posterior aspect of the uterus.
The surgeon examines the left pelvic brim, searching for the ureter where there are no adhesions. The dissection begins to separate the left fallopian tube from the ovary and adjacent bowel.
The surgeon visually identifies the stacked left fallopian tube and ovary, noting the left ovary proprium on the left side of the surgical field.
The surgeon examines the right ovary and fallopian tube, which are visibly attached to the posterior aspect of the uterus.
The surgeon observes the right fallopian tube and right ovary, which are visibly adhered to each other.
The surgeon identifies an endometrial implant within the surgical field, visually distinct from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon begins dissection in the right pararectal space, starting from the right pelvic brim. The focus is on locating the ureter on the right side where there are no adhesions.
The surgeon identifies the right ureter and carefully dissects it from the surrounding peritoneum, separating it from any adhesions.
The surgeon identifies a chocolate cyst on the right ovary and attempts to dissect the right fallopian tube from the ovary, which are visibly attached.
The surgeon focuses on the left side, carefully dissecting the ureter from adhesions involving the ovary and bowel, using precise maneuvers to separate these structures.
The surgeon dissects the parapelvic parorectal spaces, focusing on separating the ovary from the rectum using careful dissection techniques.
The surgeon observes the ovary severely attached to the rectosigmoid colon, adhering to the principle that fat belongs to the bowel during dissection.
The surgeon exposes the peritoneum and identifies the parorectal space located between the ureter and the rectum.
The surgeon observes severe adhesions attaching the ovary to the rectus sigmoid colon, with the peritoneum visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the left ureter and carefully dissects it from the surrounding peritoneum, progressing towards the uterosacral ligament insertion near the uterus.
The surgeon places a rectal probe into the rectum, attempting to visualize the ureter and uterus sacral ligament, which are obscured due to attachment to the ureter and rectus sigmoid.

The surgeon attempts to dissect the rectovaginal space, addressing strict adhesions between the torus-uterus part and the bowel. Dissection efforts are focused on creating separation from the left and right pararectal spaces.

The surgeon performs a bimanual examination to differentiate the vagina and rectum within the rectovaginal space, confirming the correct anatomical location. A rectal probe is visible, and the vaginal mucosa is palpated. The surgeon inspects the area for nodules, finding none, and observes adhesions between the rectosigmoid and the pelvic wall.

The surgeon holds and dissects the fibrotic right utero-sacral ligament from the rectum, mobilizing the rectosigmoid area. The recto-vaginal space is visible, along with the right and left utero-sacral ligaments, right tube, right ovary, and right ureter. The left ovary, left ureter, and left utero-sacral ligament are also visible, showing the surgical field after adhesiolysis.
